title: Islands
description: Interactive client components with selective hydration

blocks.text: Islands are Preact components that run in the browser. Everything else in Howl renders on the server and sends static HTML. Islands are the exception — they hydrate on the client and become interactive.
This model ("islands architecture") means you ship zero JavaScript by default. You opt in to JS per component, keeping bundles small and pages fast.
Create a file ending in .island.tsx in your islands/ directory. Use it in any page like a regular component — Howl detects it automatically.
Props passed to islands must be JSON-serializable — they are embedded in the HTML as inline JSON for hydration.

Island file names must end in `.island.tsx` (or `.island.jsx` / `.island.ts` / `.island.js`). The crawler throws at build time for any other naming inside `islands/` directories or `(_islands)` route groups. Earlier releases warned and continued, which masked silent hydration bugs.

Default islands run renderToString on the server and emit their full HTML inline. On the client, Howl calls hydrate() — Preact walks the existing DOM and attaches event listeners without wiping or re-rendering. No flash, no double paint.
When an island opts out of SSR (ssr: false), the server emits empty markers and the client uses render() to mount fresh. The hydrate-vs-render switch is automatic.
By default, islands are SSR'd. To skip server rendering entirely (render nothing until JS loads), export a howl config:
Use ssr: false for islands that depend on browser APIs (window, document, WebGL) or for third-party React libs that emit non-Preact vnodes during SSR (sonner, mapbox-gl, ...).
Pair ssr: false with a skeleton component to prevent layout shift while JS loads. The skeleton renders server-side inside the island markers; the real component replaces it on first client render.
Skeleton is most effective when its dimensions match the final component — that's the entire point. A mismatched skeleton causes more flicker than no skeleton at all.

ClientOnly defers a single child element until after the island has mounted on the client. Use it when most of the island SSRs fine but one nested component cannot — e.g. sonner's <Toaster /> inside an otherwise SSR'd interactive component.
ClientOnly is for one bad child inside an otherwise-SSR'd island. If the whole component cannot SSR, prefer ssr: false on the island instead.
Two boolean exports tell you which side of the wire you are on. Use them to short-circuit code paths that would crash during SSR.
IS_SERVER is the inverse of IS_BROWSER — pick whichever reads better at the call site.
Islands work with @preact/signals for fine-grained reactivity. Signals that change only re-render the specific DOM node they're bound to — no component re-render needed.
